findings.description: Source unit explicitly labels this as a 'CTA pattern' (implying a templated/paraphrasable structure), not a fixed verbatim string. Encoding it as hard_constraint/must with a 'verbatim' name overstates rigidity vs. the source's pattern framing; hardness/polarity should reflect that variation on the pattern is permitted (e.g., should_constraint or a distinct 'pattern' class) rather than an absolute verbatim mandate.

payload.note: reflect 'pattern' language from source, allow structural reuse rather than strict verbatim lock

findings.description: Headline makes an FDA-approval and disease-first-treatment claim; as a hard_constraint/must copy_editorial rule it lacks any governance facet (e.g., MLR/regulatory review owner, approval reference) despite being a compliance-sensitive verbatim claim.

MOA statements make clinical mechanism-of-action claims (targets root cause / turns off inflammatory switches) that are typically subject to medical/regulatory sign-off; the merged rule has no governance facet capturing required medical review or claim substantiation reference.

Product benefit bullets are efficacy/safety-adjacent claims (muscle strength, skin symptoms, steroid reliance) requiring compliance approval; rule lacks governance metadata (approval owner, review cadence) despite hard_constraint/must classification.

Single rule bundles two distinct facts: (1) the verbatim program name 'My Compass Support' and (2) the description of support services/scope (PAL communication channels, authorization support). These have different governance sensitivity (naming/branding vs. patient-support/compliance operations like authorization support) and should be split so each can carry appropriate hardness/governance facets.

Administration statement is a hard_constraint/must copy_editorial rule about dosing/administration claims, which typically requires medical/regulatory governance sign-off; no governance facet present.
